{{Short description|Atmospheric ghost lights in Japanese folklore}}
[[File:Bakemono-tsukushi-emaki Kitsune-bi.jpg|thumb|The ''[[Bakemono Tsukushi Emaki]]'', author unknown (late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}})]]
[[File:SekienKitsunebi.jpg|thumb|"Kitsunebi" from the ''{{ill|Gazu Hyakki Yagyō|en|Gazu Hyakki Yagyō|qid=Q2696876|lt=Gazu Hyakki Yagyō|1=Gazu Hyakki Yagyō}}'' by [[Toriyama Sekien]]]]
'''Kitsunebi''' (狐火) is an {{ill|Atmospheric ghost lights|en|Atmospheric ghost lights|qid=Q8786340|lt=atmospheric ghost light|1=Atmospheric ghost lights}} told about in legends all across Japan outside {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}.<ref name="murakami">村上健司編著 『妖怪事典』 毎日新聞社、2000th year 、134頁。{{ISBN|978-4-620-31428-0}}。</ref> They are also called "hitobosu", "hitomoshi" (火点し),<ref name="林">{{Harvnb|林|1977|p=5}}</ref> and "rinka" (燐火).<ref name="鈴木">{{Harvnb|鈴木|2002|pp=38–39}}</ref><ref name="yahoo">{{Cite web |title=きつねび【狐火】 |url=http://dic.yahoo.co.jp/dsearch?enc=UTF-8&p=%E7%8B%90%E7%81%AB&dtype=0&dname=0ss&stype=0&pagenum=1&index=104594600000 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090804061906/http://dic.yahoo.co.jp/dsearch?enc=UTF-8 |archive-date=2009-08-04 |access-date=2013-03-20 |website= |publisher=[[Yahoo! JAPAN]]}}</ref>

==Overview==
Kimimori Sarashina, a researcher of local stories, summarizes the features of the kitsunebi as follows: in places where there was no presence of fire, mysterious flames like those of a paper lantern or a torch would appear in a line and flicker in and out, with fires that had gone out sometimes appearing in yet another place, so that if one attempted to chase after what was behind all this, it would disappear in the middle.<ref name="根本">{{Harvnb|根本|1985|p=597}}</ref> When they appear between spring and autumn, they show up especially in hot summers and appear easily when it is cloudy when the weather is changing.<ref name="根本" />

They are said to appear from ten to several hundred in a line, and just when one thinks that they have increased, they would suddenly disappear, then multiply once again.<ref name="草野">{{Harvnb|草野|1997|p=102}}</ref> In the Nagano Prefecture, many lights like that of a paper lantern would appear in a line and flicker.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=有賀喜左衛門|date=1929-04-20|title=爐辺見聞|journal=民族|volume=4巻|issue=3号|pages=144–145|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/2240230.shtml |id={{NCID|AN00236864}}}}</ref>

The line's length spans across up to one [[Li (unit)#Other cultures|ri]] (about 500–600 m).<ref name="多田">{{Harvnb|多田|1990|pp=344–345}}</ref> Generally the color of the fire is red or orange,<ref name="草野" /> but there have been several examples of witnesses that have seen blue flames.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=土井卓治|date=November 1955 |title=伯耆大山を眺めつつ歩く|journal=あしなか|issue=通巻49号|page=22|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0030143.shtml |id={{NCID|AN00406352}}}}</ref>

Concerning their location of appearance, in {{ill|Tonami, Toyama|en|Tonami, Toyama|qid=Q526760|lt=Tonami|1=Tonami, Toyama}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|qid=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}, they said to appear in the hillsides where there are no roads and other places where there is no presence of humans;<ref name="林" /> but in {{ill|Monzen, Ishikawa|en|Monzen, Ishikawa|qid=Q6907002|lt=Monzen|1=Monzen, Ishikawa}}, {{ill|Fugeshi District, Ishikawa|en|Fugeshi District, Ishikawa|qid=Q5507254|lt=Fugeshi District|1=Fugeshi District, Ishikawa}}, {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|en|Ishikawa Prefecture|qid=Q131281|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|1=Ishikawa Prefecture}} (now {{ill|Wajima, Ishikawa|en|Wajima, Ishikawa|qid=Q823896|lt=Wajima|1=Wajima, Ishikawa}}), there are also legends where kitsunebi is said to follow a human anywhere.<ref>{{Cite journal|date=1991-01-20 |title=石川県鳳至郡門前町 調査報告書|journal=常民|issue=27号|page=75|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1070571.shtml |id={{NCID|AN00116782}}}}</ref> It is often said that foxes trick humans, so likewise, the kitsunebi would light up places where there are no roads and make the humans lose their way.<ref name="鈴木" /> In {{ill|Iida, Nagano|en|Iida, Nagano|qid=Q841129|lt=Iida|1=Iida, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, it was said to be possible to disperse it when such a thing happens by kicking it up with one's feet.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=宮澤千章|date=2003-01-01|title=火の玉と狐火|journal=伊那|volume=51巻|issue=1号（通巻896号）|page=30|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0160271.shtml |id={{NCID|AN00015559}}}}</ref> In {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}} (now {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|qid=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}), there are legends about catching a fever after encountering a kitsunebi, giving a strong basis for the hypothesis that kitsunebi are like [[ikiaigami]] (divine spirits that bring misfortune from unprepared encounters with them).<ref name="清原">{{Harvnb|清原編|2009|pp=28–29}}</ref>

Also, there was a story in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano|1=Nagano Prefecture}} in which a certain lord and vassal were looking for a place to build a castle. A white fox lit up the path at night and guided the way for them to reach a suitable place for a castle.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=石川正臣|date=1984-01-01|title=飯田の伝説 飯田の烏|journal=伊那|volume=32巻|issue=1号（通巻688号）|page=15|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/0160108.shtml |id={{NCID|AN00015559}}}}
</ref>

Just as {{ill|Masaoka Shiki|en|Masaoka Shiki|qid=Q315152|lt=Masaoka Shiki|1=Masaoka Shiki}} composed {{ill|Haiku|en|Haiku|qid=Q37707|lt=Haiku|1=Haiku}} about winter and kitsunebi, they usually appear during the winter; but there have also been examples where they appear in the hot season of summer or in autumn.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=中島繁男|date=1937-08-25|title=狐火|journal=日本民俗|volume=2巻|issue=12号|page=19|url=http://www.nichibun.ac.jp/YoukaiCard/1700006.shtml |id={{NCID|AN00018761}}}}</ref>

There is a theory that kitsunebi is another name for {{ill|Onibi|en|Onibi|qid=Q11673509|lt=Onibi|1=Onibi}},<ref name="yahoo"/> but usually they are considered separate from onibi.

==Legends by area==
===In Ōji Inari===
[[File:Hiroshige-100-views-of-edo-fox-fires.jpg|thumb|"Kitsunebi on New Year's Night under the Enoki Tree near Ōji" in the ''{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|qid=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}}'' by Hiroshige. Each fox has a kitsunebi floating close to their face.]]
[[File:Oji Kitsune no Gyoretsu4.JPG|thumb|Kitsune no Gyoretsu(Ōji 2010)]]
[[File:Oji Kitsune no Gyoretsu5.JPG|thumb|Kitsune no Gyoretsu(Ōji 2010)]]

[[Ōji Inari]] of [[Ōji (Kita, Tokyo)|Ōji]], {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|qid=Q235130|lt=Kita|1=Kita, Tokyo}}, Tokyo, is known to be the head of {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}}, it is also a famous place for kitsunebi.<ref name="角田1982">{{Harvnb|角田|1982|pp=31–32}}</ref>

Formerly, the area around Ōji was all a rural zone, and on the roadside there was a big {{ill|Celtis sinensis|en|Celtis sinensis|qid=Q706339|lt=enoki|1=Celtis sinensis}} tree. Every year, on the night of {{ill|Ōmisoka|en|Ōmisoka|qid=Q2357104|lt=Ōmisoka|1=Ōmisoka}}, the foxes of Kanhasshū (all of the Kantō region) would gather below the tree, put on uniforms, call on their Ranks, and visit the palace of Ōji Inari.<ref name="多田" /><ref name="角田1982" /><ref name="miyao">{{Harvnb|宮尾|1963|p=93}}</ref> As the kitsunebi that can be seen on this occasion was quite a spectacle, it is said that the peasants around the area would count their numbers and used that to predict a good or bad harvest for next year.<ref name="miyao" />{{Sfn|角田|1979|pp=174-178}}

From this, enoki trees are also called "shōzoku enoki" (装束榎, "costume enoki"), and it became a well known place, and even became a subject in [[Hiroshige]]'s work {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|qid=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}}. The tree withered away in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}},<ref name="withfox">{{Cite web|url=http://www.withfox.jp/guidekita/syouzokuinari.html|title=装束稲荷神社|publisher=東京の石狐めぐり|access-date=2011-08-26|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090525035501/http://www.withfox.jp/guidekita/syouzokuinari.html|archive-date=2009-05-25}}</ref> but a small shrine called the "Shōzoku Inari Jinja" remains next to the former second Ōji tram stop (now in front of the "horibun" intersection point),<ref name="withfox" /> and the area was previously called Enokimachi (榎町, "enoki town").<ref name="miyao" /> As this area was part of a larger development plan, in 1993, on the evening of the annual Ōmisoka, an event was held called the "Ōji Kitsune's Procession".<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ouji-kitsune.jp/|title=王子狐の行列 公式ホームページ|publisher=王子狐の行列の会|access-date=2013-03-20|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130325204137/http://ouji-kitsune.jp/|archive-date=2013-03-25}}</ref>

===Other places===
In the {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|qid=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province|1=Dewa Province}} in {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|qid=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} and in {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|qid=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}}, kitsunebi are called "kitsune taimatsu" (狐松明, "fox torch"). As its name implies, it is said to be a torchlight to provide lighting for a fox's marriage,<ref name="多田" /> and is said to be a good omen.<ref>{{Cite book|author=日野巌・日野綏彦|editor=[[村上健司]]校訂|title=動物妖怪譚|和書|orig-year=1926|year=2006|publisher=[[中央公論新社]]|series=[[中公文庫]]|isbn=978-4-12-204792-1|volume=下|page=256|chapter=日本妖怪変化語彙}}</ref>

In {{ill|Bizen, Okayama|en|Bizen, Okayama|qid=Q863741|lt=Bizen|1=Bizen, Okayama}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|qid=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}, and the {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|qid=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, these atmospheric ghost lights are called "'''chūko'''" (宙狐).<ref name="井上">{{Harvnb|井上|1916|p=160}}</ref><ref name="大藤">{{Harvnb|大藤他|1955|p=929}}</ref> Different from the average kitsunebi, they float at relatively low altitudes, and thus in Toyohara village, {{ill|Oku District, Okayama|en|Oku District, Okayama|qid=Q7082713|lt=Oku District|1=Oku District, Okayama}} of Okayama, it is said that an old fox shapeshifted into a chūko.<ref name="大藤" /> Similarly, on Ryūgūjima, Tamatsu village, Oku District, the atmospheric ghost lights that appear at night with signs of coming rain that are about as big as paper lanterns are called chūko. Sometimes they would fall to the earth and illuminate the surroundings, and then finally disappear without a trace.{{Sfn|村上|2000|p=219}} Enryō Inoue, a yōkai researcher from the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, applied the characters '''中狐''' to it, indicating the ones that fly high as "tenko" (天狐), and the ones that fly low as chūko.<ref name="井上" />

==True identity==
[[File:Kinmo_Tenchiben_Kitsunebi.jpg|right|thumb|240px|Depiction of kitsunebi from the ''Kunmō Tenchiben''. It depicts a fox holding a bone in its mouth and causing a fire.]]
In various folk legends and writings of the Edo Period, there are many things said about foxes concerning how their breath would glow,<ref name="鈴木1982">{{Harvnb|鈴木|1982|pp=198–199}}</ref> how they would strike with their tails to light a fire,<ref name="鈴木1982" />{{Sfn|角田|1979|p=183}} and how they would have a glowing ball called the "kitsunebi-tama" (kitsunebi ball),<ref name="菊岡">{{Harvnb|菊岡|1800|p=474}}</ref> among other tales. The {{ill|Kanpō|en|Kanpō|qid=Q1145798|lt=Kanpō|1=Kanpō}} period essay the ''Shokoku Rijidan'' states that in the beginning years of {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|qid=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}}, when fishermen capture kitsunebi with their nets, there would be a kitsunebi-tama caught in their nets, and it was an object that was useful as illumination because it does not shine during the daytime but would glow at night time.<ref name="菊岡" />

In the Genroku period book about herbalism, the ''[[Honchō Shokkan]]'', there is a statement about how foxes would use withered trees on the ground to make fires. "Fox fire" in English is translated to "kitsunebi" in Japanese, and this "fox" does not refer to the animal, but instead means "withered" or "rotten and discolored", and seeing how "fox fire" refers to the fire of withered trees and the light of {{ill|hypha|en|hypha|qid=Q193129|lt=hypha|1=Hypha}} and mushroom roots that cling to withered trees,<ref name="根本" /><ref name="神田">{{Harvnb|神田|1931|pp=15–17}}</ref> statements such as the one from the ''Honchō Shokkan'' could be seen to refer to the light from the hypha on withered trees on the ground.<ref name="神田" />

Also in the ''[[Honchō Shokkan]]'', there are statements about how foxes would make light using human skulls and horse bones, and the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|qid=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa|1=Meiwa}} period ''Kunmō Tenchiben'' by the yomihon author Takai Ranzan and the late Edo Period ''Shōzan Chomon Kishū'' by the essayist Miyoshi Shōzan also state that foxes would hold horse bones in their mouth to light a fire.<ref name="神田23">{{Harvnb|神田|1931|pp=23–25}}</ref> In the collection of strange tales from the Nagano Prefecture, the ''Shinshū Hyaku Monogatari'', when a person goes near a kitsunebi, there would be a fox holding human bones in their mouth, and after the fox goes away, the human bone would be glowing a turquoise color.<ref name="清原" /> From things such as this, Enryō Inoue among others support the theory that phosphorous light given off from within bones is linked to kitsunebi.<ref name="神田23" /> Phosphorus {{ill|spontaneous combustion|en|spontaneous combustion|qid=Q369012|lt=spontaneously combusts|1=Spontaneous combustion}} above 60 degrees, which would also be a reason for why the fox's true identity would be linked to the light from phosphorus.<ref name="清原" /> However, the kitsunebi in legends is said to be visible even from a distance of several kilometers away, which would be hard to square with the idea that they are actually sources of light as weak as hypha or phosphorus.<ref name="神田" /><ref name="神田23" />

In 1977, the folkloricist Yoshiharu Tsunda's detailed research gave the explanation that almost all kitsunebi can be explained by a large refraction of light that often occurs in alluvial fans that go between mountainous and plain regions.<ref name="根本" /> There are also other hypotheses on their true identities such as the natural combustion of petroleum or {{ill|ball lightning|en|ball lightning|qid=Q192962|lt=ball lightning|1=Ball lightning}}, but there are many that still go unexplained.<ref name="根本" />
